## blog navigation

## blog posts

## Transnational Memories: Sites, Knots, Methods

## Images

![](https://files.blogs.illinois.edu/files/9114/756308867/212003.png)
![](https://files.blogs.illinois.edu/files/9114/756308867/212005.png)
![](https://files.blogs.illinois.edu/files/9114/756308867/212005.png)

**Edit** embedded media in the Files Tab and re-insert as needed.

Debarati Sanyal, Michael Rothberg, Ann Rigney, and Rosanne Kennedy at the Transnational Memories: Sites, Knots, Memories panel at the 2015 Modern Language Association Convention

This *Days and Memory* forum brings together four scholars of cultural memory, based on three continents, who have been actively involved in promoting the transnational turn in memory studies: [Rosanne Kennedy](https://researchers.anu.edu.au/researchers/kennedy-rm) (Australian National University), [Ann Rigney](http://www.rigney.nl/) (Utrecht University), [Michael Rothberg](http://michaelrothberg.weebly.com/) (University of Illinois), and [Debarati Sanyal](http://french.berkeley.edu/professors/debarati-sanyal/) (University of California, Berkeley). Three of the presenters (Kennedy, Rigney, and Rothberg) are part of an internationally funded research project called the [Network in Transnational Memory Studies (NITMES)](http://nitmes.wp.hum.uu.nl/), which was initiated by Ann Rigney with support from the NWO (The Dutch Research Council). Through a series of conferences, faculty exchanges, and publications—including this one—NITMES seeks to provide a platform for new debates in cultural memory studies.  
  
In the past 25 years, memory studies has emerged as a new interdisciplinary field of cultural inquiry. It aims for insight into practices of public remembrance and the sociocultural dynamics through which mediations of the past shape collective identities and inform social action. The development of this field was linked from the outset to investigations of national memory cultures and institutions, with the nation‐state taken as the most self‐evident framework for analysis. Pierre Nora’s pioneering, influential, and contested study *Les Lieux de Memoire* (1984‐1992) encapsulated the link between “territorialization” (focusing on how memory narratives are fixed, located, contained, “inherited”) and the nation (as the self‐evident frame within which narratives operate and identities are shaped). With the turn towards transnational approaches in the last decade, however, Nora's concept of “sites of memory” has come under pressure as the assumed framework for memory studies. In our increasingly globalized, networked, and mediated world, alternatives to national models of memory as a resource for identity constructions are beginning to emerge without yet being fully understood.  
  
This forum explores the consequences of the transnational turn for the study of sites, practices, and *noeuds* or knots of memory. The short papers collected here—originally presented at the Modern Language Association Convention in Vancouver in January 2015 on a roundtable organized by Rosanne Kennedy and Michael Rothberg—track the intersection of memory and politics across a range of sites: from Ireland to Indonesia, from Germany to Kurdistan, and beyond. At stake in these discussions are such multivalenced categories as complicity, human rights, and internationalism, which play out in relation to what Astrid Erll has called "traveling memories." Paying close attention to a range of media—from photographs and films to literary texts and monuments—contributors interrogate the mobilizing potential of public remembrance, its catalyzing force in activist projects both within and beyond nation-states.    
  
You can find the papers here:   
  
1. [Debarati Sanyal, “Memory in Complicity”](http://hgmsblog.weebly.com/blog/memory-in-complicity)  
  
2. [Rosanne Kennedy, “*The Act of Killing*, the Global Memory Imperative, and Trans/national Accountability”](http://hgmsblog.weebly.com/blog/the-act-of-killing-the-global-memory-imperative-and-transnational-accountability)  
  
3. [Michael Rothberg, “Remembering Ronahî, Remembering Internationalism”](http://hgmsblog.weebly.com/blog/remembering-ronahi-remembering-internationalism)  
  
4. [Ann Rigney, “Transnational Bloody Sundays: Multi-Sited Memory”](http://hgmsblog.weebly.com/blog/transnational-bloody-sundays-multi-sited-memory1)  
  
We invite you to join the discussion!

Review

Narratives and collective memory
========================================

Author links open overlay panel Cesar Lopez, Floor van Alphen, Laura Carmona

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2025.102087](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2025.102087 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2352250X25001009&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Narratives should be analyzed alongside other artefacts within a shared mnemonic system. 
*   •Narratives are powerful because they accommodate change as well as repetition. 
*   •Participatory transmedia narratives provide inclusive frameworks for conflicting narratives. 
*   •Narrative approaches complement the understanding and implementation of historical thinking. 

Current research continues to emphasize the role of [narratives](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/narrative "Learn more about narratives from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as a key cultural mediator for collective memory. Recent studies examine how narratives shape collective identities—not in isolation, but by integrating them with other mediators such as symbols, monuments, or rituals. Key concepts for understanding how narratives function, such as schematic narrative templates, have been refined to account for increasingly heterogeneous and dynamic identities in societies today. The study of narratives must also consider the rise of counter- and alternative narratives that coexist with—and sometimes challenge—master narratives. Narrative approaches are making significant contributions at the intersection of memory and history, contributions that are especially relevant to fostering critical and historical thinking in our societies. Critical thinking about narratives is essential for citizens to negotiate and interrogate them, as well as generate their own. To better understand the role of narratives in collective memory it is crucial to investigate the points of tension and interrelation between their production and consumption, between memory and history, and between dominant and alternative narratives. All of this occurs in a context that presents new challenges for narrative research—such as the rise of extremist political discourse and use of national narratives, and the emergence of new narrative-production mechanisms like artificial intelligence.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25000910)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001010)

Background
----------

[Narratives](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/narrative "Learn more about Narratives from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been a key element in discussions regarding collective memory almost since the pioneering studies on this matter [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib1)]. Although collective memory is a term in constant redefinition, it revolves around the shared representation of the past by members of a group. As individuals have no immediate access to the past, the socio-cultural psychological approach places emphasis on how they actively remember through shared [cultural tools](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cultural-tools "Learn more about cultural tools from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that mediate how the past is produced, preserved, transmitted, and reproduced in the present and for the future. For these purposes, narratives constitute a central cultural mediator of collective memory [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib2)].

Socio-cultural psychology has moved forward from viewing narratives as passive repositories of collective memory to active tools used to _produce_ and _consume_ those memories [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib3)]. Production stresses the epistemological idea that narrative knowledge is actively shaped by a narrator. Consumption reveals the multiple ways in which different individuals give meaning to and respond to produced narratives. Examining the dynamics and tensions between these two offers a productive avenue for understanding the role of narratives in collective memory. What makes certain produced narratives particularly effective, resilient, and meaningful for specific groups? How do these narratives evolve into master narratives that shape the imagination of the community and define its identity? How do alternative perspectives on these master narratives arise and transform into counter-narratives? In what ways do individuals engage with these narratives, and how can we promote critical reflection on them? These questions—and others of a similar nature—have been central to recent theoretical and empirical research on narratives, bringing together fields as memory studies, history, [sociology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/sociology "Learn more about sociology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and education.

Narratives and collective identity
----------------------------------

As with the past, individuals do not have direct access to the community itself—even in the digital age of the internet and social media. [Narratives](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/narrative "Learn more about Narratives from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enable individuals and groups to imagine—and, for the sake of collective self-esteem, even to invent—their identity [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib5)]. Narratives of collective memory offer the privilege of constituting a collective subject, the “we,” positioned advantageously in opposition to “them.” [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib6)] Traditionally, narratives establishing group identities have been characterized as homogeneous and static structures that convey an [essentialist](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/essentialist "Learn more about essentialist from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and immutable vision of a collective “we.” However, recent arguments suggest that these narratives resist change precisely due to their adaptability and [dynamism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/dynamism "Learn more about dynamism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib8)]. This adaptability enables different groups to appropriate the master narrative, reshape it to fit their interests, reframing its meaning while preserving its symbolic power.

There is a growing interest in addressing the notion of myths when analyzing narratives of identity, such as national narratives [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib9),[10∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib10),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib3)]. Myths narrate the origin of the community, but they also establish a utopian horizon toward which it should strive, as well as desirable moral values, emotions and behaviors. Myths and identity narratives are also connected with other artefacts of collective memory, such as symbols or rituals. The existence of these non-text-based cultural mediators highlights the distributed nature of collective memory. Recent proposals emphasize the value of conducting research that integrates the way these different artifacts interact within a shared mnemonic system [[11∗∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib11)].

In this vein, Brockmeier [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib12)] proposed three levels of narrative integration: linguistic, semiotic, and performative. The linguistic level involves narratives expressed through oral or written language, but he highlights how material symbols convey these narratives, which ultimately transform into meaning-laden acts in commemorations and rituals within the performative order. Mecklenfeld [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib13)] exemplifies how these three levels powerfully reflect collective identity with the case of the _Kreuzkampf_ (crucifix struggle) in 1936. The _linguistic level_ recounts Catholic resistance to the Nazi order to remove crucifixes from schools and public buildings in the Oldenburger Münsterland region. The _semiotic level_ is represented by the crucifix itself as an identity symbol that inherently conveys the narrative, which is ultimately reinforced at the _performative level_ through [pilgrimages](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/pilgrimages "Learn more about pilgrimages from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), speeches, and commemorative practices. When investigating the relationship between different memory artifacts and identity narratives, it is particularly insightful to reconsider Jörn Rüsen’s [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib14)] concept of _narrative abbreviaturen_—a notion that refers to how concepts, symbols, rituals, or even simple gestures can evoke entire myths and identity-forming narratives without the need for fully narrate them.

Schematic narrative templates
-----------------------------

Investigating how collective memory is shaped, in his famous study bringing scholarship in psychology, linguistics and history together, Wertsch [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib15)] encountered the narrative pattern of _triumph over alien forces_ again and again in the rendering of different episodes of the Russian past – and, similarly, the pattern of the _quest for freedom_ in the case of the United States. He proposed the notion of _schematic narrative template (SNT)_: a generalized narrative form, following basic principles of temporality and plot, that shapes collective remembering and how – the identities of – communities are imagined. From this general structure, _specific narratives_ about particular events, dates, places and actors can be generated.

SNT’s characteristically go unnoticed, resulting in a naturalized way of representing the past with great emotional significance. Hence, they are key tools for [political mobilization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/political-mobilization "Learn more about political mobilization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and it is very hard to change these templates, even in the face of clear historical evidence or sociopolitical change. Yet, Wertsch’s sociocultural approach is not deterministic: there is an _irreducible tension_ between the remembering agent and the narrative [cultural tool](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cultural-tools "Learn more about cultural tool from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") allowing her/him to remember. Thus, people can reproduce but also resignify and resist them. For example, lay people are aware of the fact that official narratives do not properly reflect their experiences [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib16)], government officials can decide to change the template in the face of sociopolitical change [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib17)], and youth can resist homogenizing narratives [[18∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib18),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib19),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib20)].

The SNT concept has been elaborated upon and contested within and across nations worldwide. For example, Van Nieuwenhuyse and Bouhon [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib21)] indicate that in the context of a declining nation-state like Belgium there is no single dominant template and Karrouche [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib22)] points at an important ethnic-linguistic divide in Moroccan memory. Mutlutürk, Tekcan and Boduroglu [[23∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib23)] also suggest that collective memory may best be understood at the level of specific ethnic or sociopolitical groups rather than at a larger national level. The idea of neatly ordered mnemonic communities is further complicated by research on the international travelling of SNT’s [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib24), [25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib25), [26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib26)]. Though Wertsch [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib27)] emphasizes that SNT’s are not universal archetypes, but a feature of narrative traditions in specific cultural settings, recent work has pointed at the transcultural dynamics connecting different settings and traditions [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib29)].

In another critical vein, Kaidesoja [[30∗∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib30)] argues that the plot structure of narrative texts and the schematic processing by individual members of mnemonic communities should not be conflated. Both need different methods to be properly studied and a better understanding of individual and collective memory can be achieved “if cognitive scientific and social scientific explanations of overlapping memory phenomena mutually constrain each other.” Indeed, in interdisciplinary research on SNT’s, the tension between narrative production and consumption needs further articulation.

Master and alternative narratives
---------------------------------

Master narratives can be defined as culturally shared stories that shape our collective understanding of the past and guide our actions and thoughts in the present and future. For a narrative to become dominant it must be ubiquitous, useful, invisible, quite rigid and have a compulsory nature [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib31)].

Master narratives impact not only through what they include but also through what they omit, often silencing marginalized groups, as highlighted in Clark’s research [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib32)], which shows the exclusion of Indigenous communities in Australia due to their lack of contribution to the country’s “national exceptionalism”, and consequently, to the utility of the master narrative. This silence can lead to the emergence of alternative narratives that amplify underrepresented voices, either by directly contradicting the master narrative, or by responding to it in a way that simultaneously complies with and resists it [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib33)].

The creation of a master narrative is typically attributed to [power structures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/power-structure "Learn more about power structures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that promote it through institutionalization. However, responsibility for their [perpetuation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/perpetuation "Learn more about perpetuation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is shared between the context, such as an intractable conflict that gives rise to a conflict-supporting master narrative [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib34)]; and people themselves, who may lack the agency to resist. Even when resisting, as Hochman and Spector-Mersel [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib33)] and McLean and Syed [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib31)] note, their strategies can unintentionally reinforce the master narrative, as these strategies often involve recognizing its presence, whether explicitly or implicitly, when offering an alternative.

According to McLean and Syed [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib31)], there are two ways in which people engage with master narratives: through [internalization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/internalization "Learn more about internalization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), in which individuals, due to the metanarrative’s invisibility, are unaware of their compliance and thus, passively support it, especially when their identities align with it [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib35)]; and through negotiation, which involves active resistance, although this can risk one’s identity, as it is shaped by the very narrative being challenged, and may lead to the creation of an alternative narrative that conflicts with the dominant one.

To establish alternative narratives, Basaraba and Cauvin [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib36)∗∗] stress balancing top-down and bottom-up approaches. They argue that both historians and the public should contribute sources and interpretations of historical events to prevent institutional control from silencing perspectives, or ending up with fragmented, disconnected stories due to excessive public trust. They promote diverse historical interpretations coexisting through transmedia, rather than aiming for consensus like master narratives. Reynolds [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib37)] also supports this approach, referring to it as agonistic memory [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib38)], in public history, using oral testimonies from individuals with contested narratives about the 1968 [civil rights movement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/civil-rights-movement "Learn more about civil rights movement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in Northern Ireland. The same event was studied by Barton [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib39)] in the context [of history education](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/history-of-education "Learn more about of history education from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), where he observed that teachers presented both perspectives in class, believing it would foster a more balanced and academic understanding of history for students.

Narratives and history education
--------------------------------

In [history education](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/history-education "Learn more about history education from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), national narratives continue to be the quintessential master narratives. Recent studies indicate that this strong presence is produced both at narrative production—especially through textbooks—and student consumption [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib40),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib41),[42∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib42),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib43)]. However, an increasing number of approaches seek to question and deconstruct these national narratives.

First, a pronounced current exists to promote what is referred to as _historical thinking_. These proposals, fundamentally cognitive in nature, encourage reflection on problems encountered by historians when analyzing the past. Competencies such as _historical perspective_, _evidence_, understanding _change and continuity_, discerning _causes and consequences_, assessing _historical significance_, and comprehending the _ethical dimension_ of history have been routinely addressed for the last two decades [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib44), [45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib45), [46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib46)].

Second, the growing presence of counter-narratives—partly due to increasingly complex and heterogeneous societies—and the social debates they provoke, challenge education to incorporate them and require students to develop the competences to critically examine and negotiate these narratives [[47∗](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib47)]. To address these, new challenges have been proposed that focus, on one hand, on the inclusion of _dialogue_ as a mechanism for reflection and learning [[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib48)], and, on the other, on the incorporation of [new media](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/new-media "Learn more about new media from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to promote historical thinking. Mediators such as video games, artificial intelligence, and documentaries are considered potential educational tools for challenging traditional national narratives [[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib49),[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib50)].

Finally, narrative approaches focused on national narratives have identified six dimensions deemed necessary for fostering a critical narrative [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib51),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib52)]. First, the question is raised as to who constitutes the _historical subject_ of the narrative, thereby calling for a complexity that includes different voices and groups. Second, efforts are made to deconstruct the “_we versus them_” narratives that typically simplify history into a tale of “good versus evil.” Third, the ethical and _[moral judgments](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/moral-judgment "Learn more about moral judgments from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ underpinning these narratives are subject to scrutiny. Fourth, the historicity of _key concepts_ that support these national narratives—such as the very notions of nation and national identity—is taken into account, with a view to challenging [essentialist](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/essentialist "Learn more about essentialist from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") conceptions held by students. Fifth, the _emotional aspects_ triggered during the consumption of these national narratives, which affect both evaluations and behavior, are considered. Finally, the role of the aforementioned _schematic narrative templates_ as shapers of thought is analyzed. These six dimensions, without claiming to be closed or exhaustive, can contribute to incorporating _hot_ elements of cognition into the development of historical thinking, such as identity and emotions, and also to emphasizing narratives not only as content but as a fundamental means of critically engaging with the past.

Conclusion
----------

Current research on narratives faces new challenges, stemming in part from the increasingly complex and shifting nature of the societies we live in, as well as from new media—e.g., AI—through which narratives are produced and consumed. These challenges reflect the inherently dynamic and changing nature of collective memories and identities, as well as of narratives themselves as fundamental mediators. Paradoxically, this complex and shifting landscape is witnessing a strong resurgence of conservative [nationalist](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/nationalists "Learn more about nationalist from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") movements across various parts of the world.

Despite this new and unfamiliar scenario, we maintain that the classical idea of the [human being](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/human-being "Learn more about human being from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as a storytelling animal remains valid [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352250X25001009#bib53)]. However, today the challenge seems to lie in analyzing and critically engage narratives through an integrative lens—one that acknowledges and explores the tensions and interactions between concepts such as memory and history, master narratives and counter-narratives, or national and transnational identities.

--------------------

**Cesar Lopez:** Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing. **Floor van Alphen:** Conceptualization, Writi

enriched_points.expanded_content: Transnational memory fosters the exchange of narratives across borders, enriching cultural identities through shared histories and collective experiences, as seen in diverse diaspora communities.
Migration alters individual and collective identities, merging diverse cultural memories, which often leads to the creation of hybrid identities that reflect both origins and new influences.
Diasporic communities act as vital conduits for cultural memory, preserving and transmitting diverse narratives that challenge dominant national stories, thus enriching the global cultural landscape.
